strategic_capability_mapping.relationship: Turns an approved replace_file proposal into an actual runtime-owned workspace mutation under explicit apply gates.
Keeps workspace mutation authority in Rust with approval freshness, expected target hash, path safety, symlink, and file-kind gates.
Records apply outcomes as bounded structured ledger metadata without storing raw replacement content or full diffs.
Gives unattended callers a deterministic apply result they can use to continue, retry safely, or stop on a bounded failure reason.

questions.question: Which strategic capabilities does this phase advance?
What new user-visible, operator-visible, or runtime capability does it add?
Why can existing functionality not substitute for this work?
What concrete product gap remains if this is not implemented?
Is this semantically distinct from the previous three phases?
Can this be done without adding a new RPC?
Can this be done without adding a new summary surface?
What runtime or development-control behavior actually changes?
How does this help headless autonomous development?
How does this move the milestone toward completion?

answers.strategic_capabilities: Advances controlled_workspace_tools, runtime_permission_enforcement, persistent_structured_ledger, and headless_autonomous_development by adding the first bounded runtime-owned workspace mutation path.
answers.new_capability: A caller can apply one approved replace_file proposal to one existing regular UTF-8 workspace file through proposal.apply, with explicit authorization, target hash verification, atomic replacement, post-write verification, and a bounded apply-result ledger event.
answers.why_existing_functionality_is_insufficie: Existing proposal, approval, preflight, readiness, capability, dry-run, audit, and review RPCs only inspect or summarize state; none can mutate a workspace file after verifying the approved proposal against the latest target.
answers.product_gap_if_skipped: Brownie would remain permanently stuck at no-write readiness reports and could not complete the Product Charter path from reading and planning to safely changing workspace files under Rust authority.
answers.non_duplicative: Yes. The previous three phases were R2.1 guard activation plus M5 closeout/planning artifacts; this phase changes runtime behavior by performing a real bounded replace_file mutation.
answers.no_new_rpc: No. Existing RPCs deliberately preserve no-write behavior. Reusing them would conflate inspection, approval, or dry-run with a side-effecting operation and would weaken replay safety.
answers.no_new_summary: No. The side-effecting RPC needs a bounded apply result so headless callers can distinguish applied, denied, and failed outcomes without raw content exposure.
answers.runtime_behavior_change: The Rust runtime creates a temporary sibling file, writes bounded replacement content, flushes and syncs, atomically replaces the target, verifies the post-write SHA-256, and records WorkspacePatchApplyResultRecorded.
answers.headless_autonomous_contribution: Headless callers receive a deterministic apply status, consumed authorization flag, failed and blocked gate names, target hashes, and temp cleanup status so they can continue or retry safely without scraping raw ledger content.
answers.milestone_progress: This is the first vertical slice of M6 Controlled Apply Execution and establishes the core apply path that later phases can extend to creation, deletion, multi-file transactions, and verification workflows.

reject_when: The PR only adds reports, readiness wrappers, digests, previews, or inspection surfaces without mutating a file.
proposal.apply can run without an approved current unconsumed proposal.
proposal.apply can run without explicit authorization or expected target SHA-256 verification.
The implementation consumes authorization before successful atomic replacement and post-write verification.
The implementation can create files, delete files, mutate directories, follow symlinks, traverse parents, touch protected paths, or mutate multiple files.
The implementation stores or returns raw replacement content, raw file content, raw diffs, raw input JSON, canonical paths, absolute paths, commands, stdout, stderr, environment values, or secrets.
Tests only check type or token presence and do not prove successful replace_file mutation plus failure invariants.

guard_engine_change_review.review_intent: M6.1 updates the current phase manifest and archives the M6.1 manifest. Review must confirm the manifest describes a real bounded runtime mutation capability and does not relax the generic phase-value guard.

exit_criteria: proposal.apply exists as a side-effecting Rust runtime RPC for replace_file proposals.

Successful apply requires Approved and Valid proposal state, explicit authorize=true, current approval, fresh latest preflight, expected target SHA-256, safe workspace-relative path, regular UTF-8 file target, non-symlink target, bounded replacement content, and matching approved diff metadata.

Successful apply creates a temporary sibling file, writes bounded content, flushes and syncs, atomically replaces the target, verifies post-write SHA-256, and records WorkspacePatchApplyResultRecorded.
Failure and denial paths preserve the original file whenever possible, clean temporary files, avoid consuming authorization before verified success, and record bounded apply result metadata.

RPC responses and ledger payloads expose bounded ids, statuses, hashes, counts, and check summaries only, with no raw content, raw diffs, raw input JSON, canonical paths, absolute paths, command output, environment values, or secrets.
Behavior tests prove successful replacement, one-time authorization denial, missing authorization denial, expected hash mismatch, protected path or symlink rejection, and no raw content exposure.
VSIX protocol and runtime client accept the bounded apply result and can call proposal.apply without moving policy authority out of Rust.

No diagnostics wrapper, digest, history, readiness-only endpoint, shell execution, git execution, test execution, network, service control, file creation, file deletion, directory mutation, arbitrary rename, or multi-file transaction is added.
